visualizations.title: The Scientific Method, Step by Step

nodes.label:
Question
(Why do things float or sink?)

visualizations.title: Density: The Rule Behind Floating and Sinking
visualizations.description: The hypothesis from the floating/sinking example, written as a formula

steps.explanation: Density is just how much mass is packed into a given volume.
If the object is less dense than the fluid it's in, it floats. This is the conclusion the experiment supports.
If it's denser than the fluid, it sinks. Weight alone doesn't decide it — density does.

visualizations.title: Testing an Object (The Experiment Step)
visualizations.description: A spinning test object, like the one you'd drop in water during an experiment

visualizations.title: Density Decides: Float or Sink
visualizations.description: Two objects of different density dropped in water — the low-density one floats, the high-density one sinks

visualizations.title: More Experiments, More Confidence
visualizations.description: Confidence in your conclusion rises quickly at first, then levels off the more experiments you run

visualizations.title: The Loop in Plain Words
visualizations.description: A quick recap of the scientific method loop

spec.body_markdown:
## The Scientific Method

It's a loop, not a straight line:

1. **Question** - something you're curious about (why do things float?)
2. **Hypothesis** - your best guess at the answer (maybe it's density?)
3. **Experiment** - test the guess with real objects and real observations
4. **Analyze** - look at what actually happened, find the pattern
5. **Conclude** - state what you learned, even if it means your first guess was wrong

If the hypothesis turns out wrong, that's not a failure - it's the loop working. You go back, adjust the hypothesis, and test again. That's how every idea in physics got refined, from gravity to atoms.

visualizations.title: Float or Sink Lab
visualizations.description: Drag the density slider and watch whether the test object floats or sinks in water

visualizations.title: Being Wrong Is Part of the Process
visualizations.description: How a rejected hypothesis feeds back into a better one

nodes.label:
Hypothesis matches data
-> keep it, test more

Hypothesis doesn't match
-> that's data too, revise it
